unit.name: Robot perception and the perception-action loop
unit.description: Explains what makes robot perception different from standard computer vision: multimodal sensing, incomplete and dynamic worlds, affordances, and tight coupling between perception and action.
unit.summary: Robot perception is presented as the problem of distilling structured, decision-relevant knowledge from high-dimensional observations such as RGB, RGBD, and tactile signals [ts=775s] [ts=793s]. Li argues that the real world is messy: sensors are partial and noisy, actions can fail, and robots must cope with deformable objects, moving agents, and changing scenes [ts=817s] [ts=836s] [ts=854s]. Because of this, capable systems use multimodal sensing and combine modalities such as vision, touch, audio, and depth so that each compensates for the others [ts=877s] [ts=901s] [ts=923s]. He then contrasts passive computer vision with robot vision, which must reason about affordances and the effects of actions; robot vision is embodied, active, and situated, and should close a task-specific perception-action loop rather than recover the entire world state [ts=938s] [ts=989s] [ts=998s] [ts=1075s].

key_points.text: Robot perception turns raw RGB, RGBD, and tactile observations into structured information useful for decision making.
Perception must handle occlusion, sensing errors, failed grasps, and dynamic environments with deformable objects and other agents.
Robotic systems often fuse vision, touch, audio, and depth because the modalities provide complementary information.
Robot vision must understand affordances and the effect of actions, not only segment static images.
Robot vision is embodied, active, and environmentally situated.
Perception should focus on task-relevant local regions, like the area near a shirt button, to close the perception-action loop.

kp_catalog.name: Multimodal robot perception
kp_catalog.description: Robots need to fuse multiple sensory streams such as vision, depth, touch, and audio to recover task-relevant structure from partial, noisy observations.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because multimodal robot perception clarifies robots need to fuse multiple sensory streams such as vision, depth, touch, and audio to recover task-relevant structure from partial, noisy observations, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.

kp_catalog.name: Embodied, active, and situated robot vision
kp_catalog.description: Robot vision differs from passive computer vision because the robot has a body, can choose viewpoints or perturbations, and operates in the here-and-now of a specific physical context.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because embodied, active, and situated robot vision clarifies robot vision differs from passive computer vision because the robot has a body, can choose viewpoints or perturbations, and operates in the here-and-now of a specific physical context, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.

kp_catalog.name: Perception-action integration
kp_catalog.description: Robots should sense only what is necessary for the current task and continuously couple perception to action rather than fully reconstructing the world at all times. Robotics is framed as the closure of the loop between seeing and doing, requiring both perception and action planning.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because perception-action integration clarifies robots should sense only what is necessary for the current task and continuously couple perception to action rather than fully reconstructing the world at all times, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices though roles split between capstone and supporting.
